questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: how does TCP differ from UDP?

answers.text: The practical answer is: TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream.

answers.text: My answer: UDP datagrams can be lost, duplicated, or reordered.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. UDP datagrams can be lost, duplicated, or reordered.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. UDP datagrams can be lost, duplicated, or reordered.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: UDP avoids connection setup and lets applications choose their own reliability strategy.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. UDP avoids connection setup and lets applications choose their own reliability strategy.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. UDP avoids connection setup and lets applications choose their own reliability strategy.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what does DNS normally resolve?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: It encrypts every application connection automatically.

feedback.legit: Not this one. DNS resolution and transport encryption are separate mechanisms.
feedback.sus: Good catch. DNS resolution and transport encryption are separate mechanisms.

answers.text: From what I've seen, resolvers may cache answers until their time-to-live expires.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Resolvers may cache answers until their time-to-live expires.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Resolvers may cache answers until their time-to-live expires.

answers.text: The practical answer is: names such as hostnames into records including IP addresses.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Names such as hostnames into records including IP addresses.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Names such as hostnames into records including IP addresses.

questions.prompt: Forum check: what does idempotent mean for an HTTP method?

answers.text: From what I've seen, an idempotent request must return byte-for-byte identical responses every time.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Idempotency concerns the intended server effect; response content can change between requests.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Idempotency concerns the intended server effect; response content can change between requests.

answers.text: The practical answer is: repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once.

answers.text: My answer: The request has no side effects.

feedback.legit: Not this one. An idempotent request can change state; repetition should not add further intended change.
feedback.sus: Good catch. An idempotent request can change state; repetition should not add further intended change.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: what does a TLS server certificate help a client verify?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: that a trusted issuer bound a public key to the requested identity.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. That a trusted issuer bound a public key to the requested identity.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. That a trusted issuer bound a public key to the requested identity.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, certificates authenticate identity and keys, not application correctness.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Certificates authenticate identity and keys, not application correctness.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Certificates authenticate identity and keys, not application correctness.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: The hostname and validity period are part of normal verification.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The hostname and validity period are part of normal verification.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The hostname and validity period are part of normal verification.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what does network address translation commonly do?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Provide end-to-end encryption by itself.

feedback.legit: Not this one. NAT changes addressing; encryption requires a protocol such as TLS or IPsec.
feedback.sus: Good catch. NAT changes addressing; encryption requires a protocol such as TLS or IPsec.

answers.text: From what I've seen, nAT is a complete firewall that makes separate traffic policy unnecessary.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Address translation is not a substitute for explicit firewall and access-control rules.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Address translation is not a substitute for explicit firewall and access-control rules.

answers.text: The practical answer is: rewrite address and often port information between network realms.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Rewrite address and often port information between network realms.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Rewrite address and often port information between network realms.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: what does an IP subnet prefix length describe?

answers.text: Short version: A host can compare the destination with its on-link prefixes when making a routing decision.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A host can compare the destination with its on-link prefixes when making a routing decision.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A host can compare the destination with its on-link prefixes when making a routing decision.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Which leading address bits identify the network prefix.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Which leading address bits identify the network prefix.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Which leading address bits identify the network prefix.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: the maximum TCP port number.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Ports belong to the transport layer and are unrelated to the subnet prefix.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Ports belong to the transport layer and are unrelated to the subnet prefix.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: what does ARP do on an IPv4 local network?

answers.text: Short version: Map an IPv4 address to a link-layer address.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Map an IPv4 address to a link-layer address.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Map an IPv4 address to a link-layer address.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Resolve public domain names globally.

feedback.legit: Not this one. DNS resolves names; ARP operates on a local link.
feedback.sus: Good catch. DNS resolves names; ARP operates on a local link.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: aRP requests are normally broadcast on the local network segment.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. ARP requests are normally broadcast on the local network segment.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. ARP requests are normally broadcast on the local network segment.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: how do flow control and congestion control differ?

answers.text: The way I understand it: congestion control reacts to capacity and contention in the network.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Congestion control reacts to capacity and contention in the network.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Congestion control reacts to capacity and contention in the network.

answers.text: Short version: They are two names for the same receiver buffer setting.

feedback.legit: Not this one. They address different bottlenecks even though both can limit sending rate.
feedback.sus: Good catch. They address different bottlenecks even though both can limit sending rate.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Flow control protects the receiver from being overwhelmed.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Flow control protects the receiver from being overwhelmed.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Flow control protects the receiver from being overwhelmed.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what does browser CORS enforcement control?

answers.text: The practical answer is: CORS by itself prevents cross-site request forgery against the server.

feedback.legit: Not this one. CORS mainly controls browser access to responses; CSRF needs its own defenses.
feedback.sus: Good catch. CORS mainly controls browser access to responses; CSRF needs its own defenses.

answers.text: My answer: Whether frontend code may read certain cross-origin responses.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Whether frontend code may read certain cross-origin responses.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Whether frontend code may read certain cross-origin responses.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Whether a non-browser client can send packets to the server.

feedback.legit: Not this one. CORS is a browser security policy, not a network firewall.
feedback.sus: Good catch. CORS is a browser security policy, not a network firewall.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: why does QUIC run over UDP?

answers.text: I'd go with this: It can implement transport features in user space while using UDP datagrams for network traversal.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It can implement transport features in user space while using UDP datagrams for network traversal.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It can implement transport features in user space while using UDP datagrams for network traversal.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: it gives up reliable ordered streams entirely.

feedback.legit: Not this one. QUIC implements reliability and multiple streams above UDP.
feedback.sus: Good catch. QUIC implements reliability and multiple streams above UDP.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, independent streams reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking between streams.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Independent streams reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking between streams.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Independent streams reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking between streams.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what changes after an HTTP connection is upgraded to WebSocket?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Every message becomes an independent HTTP request.

feedback.legit: Not this one. WebSocket uses its own framing after the handshake.
feedback.sus: Good catch. WebSocket uses its own framing after the handshake.

answers.text: From what I've seen, the long-lived connection still needs failure detection and reconnection logic.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The long-lived connection still needs failure detection and reconnection logic.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The long-lived connection still needs failure detection and reconnection logic.

answers.text: The practical answer is: client and server can exchange framed messages in both directions.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Client and server can exchange framed messages in both directions.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Client and server can exchange framed messages in both directions.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: what problem does path MTU discovery address?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, path MTU discovery matters only for IPv4 and never for IPv6.

feedback.legit: Not this one. IPv6 also relies on path MTU discovery because routers do not fragment packets in transit.
feedback.sus: Good catch. IPv6 also relies on path MTU discovery because routers do not fragment packets in transit.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: The largest MTU on any link is automatically safe for the whole path.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The path MTU is constrained by the smallest supported MTU along the route.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The path MTU is constrained by the smallest supported MTU along the route.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Choosing the fastest DNS resolver.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Path MTU concerns packet size, not name resolution.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Path MTU concerns packet size, not name resolution.
